g.data-narration: Welcome to Extensions to the Basic Turing Machine. We'll explore powerful enhancements to Turing's foundational model.
g.text: Extensions to the Basic Turing Machine

g.data-narration: The basic Turing Machine is theoretically powerful but can be enhanced for convenience, efficiency, and modeling different computational paradigms.
g.text: Why Extend the Basic Turing Machine?
The basic TM is theoretically complete but impractical for certain tasks.
Extensions provide: convenience in construction, better time complexity,
and alternative frameworks for understanding computation.

g.data-narration: We will explore Multi-tape TMs, Non-deterministic TMs, Universal TMs, Linear Bounded Automata, and the Church-Turing Thesis.
A Multi-tape TM uses k tapes, each with independent read-write heads. The transition function reads from all tapes simultaneously and writes to multiple tapes.
g.text: 7-tuple: (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject)

g.data-narration: A single-tape DTM can simulate any k-tape MTM by encoding multiple tapes on one tape using special separators. This takes O(n²) time.
An NDTM has a transition function that returns a SET of possible actions. For each state-symbol pair, multiple transitions are possible.
g.text: P denotes the power set (set of all subsets)
Accepts if ANY path leads to accept state

g.data-narration: Every NDTM can be simulated by a DTM using breadth-first search. The DTM explores all computation branches systematically.
A Universal Turing Machine can simulate ANY other Turing machine. It takes both the machine description and input as its input.
g.text: UTM is the basis of stored-program computers!

g.data-narration: An LBA is a restricted TM that can only use space equal to the input length. The head cannot move beyond input boundaries marked with ⊢ and ⊣.
The Church-Turing Thesis states that any intuitively computable function can be computed by a Turing Machine. This is a thesis, not a theorem.
g.text: Any algorithm can run on TM

g.data-narration: A problem is decidable if an algorithm exists to solve it. The Halting Problem is undecidable—no algorithm can determine if an arbitrary program halts.
g.text: Given (M, w) - does M accept w?
Cannot be decided by any TM!

g.data-narration: In summary: Multi-tape and Non-deterministic TMs are computationally equivalent to basic TMs. LBAs recognize context-sensitive languages. The Church-Turing Thesis defines computability.
Remember: Extensions don't increase computational power—they provide convenience. The Turing Machine remains the definitive model of computation.
g.text: Key Insight: Extensions provide convenience, NOT additional computational power!
Turing Machine remains the gold standard for computability theory
